content:
Hello everyone :robot:,
here you can find an archive of all the public streams of this chatroom.
It's public so if you have to link to/cite a message or thread here, you can permalink that.
It gets updated automatically every hour or so, here's the magic spell making it happen (forked from here)
You did it! Great work Matteo :heart:
Yay! I suggest announcing this on #general, because it could be useful to anyone
Next we get to have a debate about the right way to cite it

Matteo Capucci (he/him) said:

Hello everyone :robot:,
here you can find an archive of all the public streams of this chatroom.
It's public so if you have to link to/cite a message or thread here, you can permalink that.

OMG man this is great
So we can link to that site if we want to reference conversations happened here in a paper?
I guess so!

For what it's worth, the Julia Zulip had a lengthy conversation - still ongoing actually - about archiving streams and, as a result, we are currently just archiving specific, well delineated, streams. For example, we have a #helpdesk, which is not archived, and a separate #helpdesk (published), which is archived publicly. Especially since this Zulip is not public. Some people might not appreciate having their comments available publicly. It is probably fine, but it might also be worth having a conversation before making all public streams archived / published.
This Zulip is public for all intents and purposes: the only reason there's not a public invite link is to reduce the possibility of spam.

Nathanael Arkor said:

This Zulip is public for all intents and purposes: the only reason there's not a public invite link is to reduce the possibility of spam.

And crackpots, I imagine...

Hi Nathanael. True. I understand that. The Julia Zulip is even more public than this CT Zulip, but we still felt it was worth having a conversation about whether all public streams should be published and searchable by Google.

Our original motivation for publishing stream archives was because we wanted certain streams, e.g. #helpdesk, to be indexed and searchable via Google. If there are no privacy concerns (most of us use our real names here) and although this Zulip is practically public, that is a little different than making everything here indexed and searchable by Google. I know I've said some personal things here, maybe I shouldn't have, thinking that I was in a community of scientists, and would have thought twice before saying if I knew we were going to open up everything to Google (who already knows too much about us).

I think Eric is raising an important issue. Indeed, even if the Zulip server is somewhat public, the feeling of intimacy is definitely here.
I personally feel the pros are more than the cons here, and that since this is, at then end of the day, a 'workspace', people should write and behave in publicly acceptable ways with or without a Googlable archive keeping them accountable to the rest of the world.
That said.
I can hide streams from the archive. So we could think about not archiving some of the less technical streams (#general, #general: meta, #general: off-topic), and even have some 'dark pit of oblivion' where we could move streams we don't want to archive.
Also let me remark that, while the internet remembers, the archive itself does not: if you edit/delete a message, that action is gonna be mirrored in the archive in the following 50 minutes.
There's some serious things to say about this. Possibly they should be said on general instead of hidden away on meta
One major consideration is to avoid becoming the new "somebody said it at a conference in Buffalo in 1968"

Jules Hedges said:

One major consideration is to avoid becoming the new "somebody said it at a conference in Buffalo in 1968"

During the lunch break on a napkin in at a diner

Jules Hedges said:

There's some serious things to say about this.

<snarky> Indeed, that's why I originally RFC-ed it on #admin: moderators :upside_down: </snarky>

Jules Hedges said:

One major consideration is to avoid becoming the new "somebody said it at a conference in Buffalo in 1968"

Isn't this an argument in favor of the archive?
Yes, it is avoided by creating the archive

Eric Forgy said:

I know I've said some personal things here, maybe I shouldn't have, thinking that I was in a community of scientists, and would have thought twice before saying if I knew we were going to open up everything to Google (who already knows too much about us).

I agree with this. Somehow I missed the vote for "should we be indexed on google".

This is one of the most civilized discussion forums I have seen. I think nobody will be harmed by their writings being more widely available. The only pain one may have could be by discovering at a later point in time, having reached a higher level of mathematical maturity, how little one knew at a previous time. But that happens to all at all stages of life in all domains, and no progress can be made if one is worries about that too much, even if just a little is good :-)

Simon Burton said:

I agree with this. Somehow I missed the vote for "should we be indexed on google".

If any, there should be a vote for not be indexed by Google. The crawlers do their things unless you stop them.
But I see your point, people

To be fair, while the participation was not especially high, it was voted that this forum should be made publicly readable. If I can't find something by googling it, I wouldn't feel like it was public.

I think it’s okay for this to be public. There’s definitely a cooling effect — There are things I’d say in person that I’d never think about saying here — but I’d guess it won’t be too bad.

I don’t think we should cite conversations here as anything other than “private communication” (or maybe “public communication”). Does anyone really think the archive will survive for, say, 30 years?
Relevant SMBC

wow this is great, thanks for doing this Matteo. have we made a "main webpage" yet, or is it just the calendar so far? I planned to make a page with guides and links to other ACT things and related communities. (apologies I haven't been active)
Mmmh I don't think there's one yet. The archive homepage is customizable though, so we could use that.
I think that could be good.

I tweeted about the recent thread The Epistemic Coup and noticed that the links pointing to community streams are not rewritten to point to the archived version of the stream. So for example I point to the discussion on Web-Cats, but that brings one to the Zulip Channel for Web-Cats which is password protected, when I think it should point to the archives of web cats.

"The Coup We Are Not Talking About" by Shoshana Zuboff via @johncarlosbaez (with discussions on Category Theory Zulip https://mattecapu.github.io/ct-zulip-archive/stream/229451-general:-off-topic/topic/The.20epistemic.20coup.html ) https://www.nytimes.com/2021/01/29/opinion/sunday/facebook-surveillance-society-technology.html

- The 🐟‍‍ BabelFish (@bblfish)
Mmh right. I didn't write the software which creates the archive but I can send a PR to who did. Certainly internal link rewriting seems like a good idea
